cataloging_subject.label: The Knight of the Tower
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloged work is the anonymous earlier Middle English prose translation of Geoffroy IV de La Tour-Landry's French Livre pour l'enseignement de ses filles, known only from British Library Harley MS 1764. It is not Geoffroy's French work, William Caxton's separate 1483 translation printed in 1484, Harley MS 1764 as a physical object, Thomas Wright's composite 1868/1906 edition, the University of Michigan encoding, or the repository XML. Because Harley 1764 has two internal lacunae and lacks its ending, Wright's edition and the electronic text insert passages from Caxton's distinct translation; those supplied passages are edition-level content and are not silently attributed to the anonymous translator.

catalog_summary.title: The Knight of the Tower
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous Middle English translator; translated from Geoffroy IV de La Tour-Landry
catalog_summary.editor: Thomas Wright; 1906 revision overseen by F. J. Furnivall
catalog_summary.date: Probably c. 1450; the sole witness is dated 1425-1474
catalog_summary.region: England; exact place of translation unknown

notes: The current British Library catalog uses this concise title for the anonymous earlier translation.

titles.value: The Book of the Knight of La Tour-Landry
notes: Wright's edition title. The carrier also includes passages from Caxton's separate translation, so the title alone does not define a homogeneous witness text.

titles.value: Livre pour l'enseignement de ses filles. English
notes: Legacy U-M uniform title preserving the relationship to the French source. It does not distinguish this anonymous translation from Caxton's later English version.

agents.name: Anonymous Middle English translator
notes: No name or secure biographical identity is known.

agents.name: Geoffroy IV de La Tour-Landry
notes: Author of the French source work, not of the anonymous Middle English wording.

agents.name: William Caxton
notes: Translator and printer of a distinct later English version used by Wright to fill defects in Harley MS 1764.

scope.description: The earlier Middle English translation as preserved incompletely in Harley MS 1764
Passages imported into Wright's composite edition where Harley MS 1764 is illegible, lacunose, or truncated

notes: This responsibility belongs only to the supplied edition passages, not to the cataloged anonymous translation as a whole.

scope.description: The 1868 edition and the inherited editorial text revised in 1906
Oversight, proof correction, additional notes, and foreword for the 1906 revised edition

notes: The Middle English Dictionary conventionally dates the text ?c1450. No normalized range is imposed because the witness's 1425-1474 production range cannot supply a logical lower bound for the earlier act of translation.
This dates production of the extant manuscript, not necessarily the act of translation.

date_statements.display: First printed in Thomas Wright's EETS edition in 1868
notes: The first edition was composite where Harley lacked text; the anonymous translation itself had not previously been printed.
The English translation and the English production of its sole witness support a broad English cultural origin. No reviewed evidence establishes an exact place of translation.

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: French and Latin in the XML occur in Wright's modern introduction and notes. They are edition-layer quotation languages, not additional discovery languages of the anonymous Middle English work.

form_statements.description: A prose translation organized as a prologue and moral exempla. The prologue explicitly says that the book was set in prose rather than rhyme.

summaries.value: An anonymous fifteenth-century English translation of Geoffroy IV de La Tour-Landry's instructional book for his daughters. A father frames a long series of biblical, legendary, historical, and social exempla as counsel on virtue and vice, worship, reputation, dress, speech, courtship, marriage, household behavior, and the dangers of misconduct. The work is prose and is directed especially toward the moral and social education of young gentlewomen.

Harley MS 1764 is the only known surviving copy of this earlier translation. It lacks leaves after fols. 24 and 32 and ends imperfectly at fol. 54v. Wright filled those gaps and the missing ending from Caxton's separate translation, so the 1868/1906 edition and KntTour-L XML are composite representations rather than complete transcriptions of the anonymous translation.

The XML preserves the revised edition's title page, Furnivall foreword, Wright introduction, medieval text on printed pp. 1-205, and 102 NOTE1 footnotes. It omits the printed table of contents and the back matter credited to Jack Munro: additional notes, glossary, phrases and proverbs, and subject index. The header sentence saying that front matter and notes were not included is therefore not an accurate description of the file as preserved.

extent.description: The manifest counts one intellectual prose work. Structurally, the XML has 135 DIV1 elements: two modern front-matter divisions and 133 body divisions comprising one prologue, 131 chapter containers, and one Caxton colophon. The edition's headings reach Chapter CXLIV, but combined headings, omitted chapter numbers, a duplicated Chapter CXX label, and the composite witness repairs mean that 144 is an editorial numbering endpoint rather than 144 independent works or one-to-one XML containers. The file also contains six DIV2, 186 P, 218 PB, 259 MILESTONE, and 102 NOTE1 elements.

content_parts.label: Furnivall's 1906 foreword
content_parts.summary: A modern foreword explaining Rossiter's transcription, Caxton supplies, Brock's corrections, the 1906 proofing and revision, and Munro's added back matter.
notes: Modern edition paratext, not part of the medieval work.

content_parts.label: Wright's historical and textual introduction
content_parts.summary: A modern introduction on Geoffroy, the French work, its translations, the anonymous earlier English version, Harley MS 1764, and Wright's decision to fill manuscript defects from Caxton.
notes: Modern edition paratext, not part of the medieval work.

content_parts.label: Anonymous earlier Middle English translation
content_parts.summary: The prologue and surviving conduct-book text transcribed principally from Harley MS 1764, excluding the passages that Wright imported from Caxton.

locators.value: ff. 1r-54v, with leaves missing after ff. 24v and 32v and an incomplete ending
pp. 1-205 except local Caxton repair on p. 1 and the supplied spans on pp. 72-75, 99-101, and 167-205

content_parts.label: Passages supplied from Caxton's separate translation
content_parts.summary: Four edition repairs: a small bracketed opening supply where Harley fol. 1r is difficult to read; the missing leaf after fol. 24v; the missing material after fol. 32v; and the complete continuation after Harley ends at fol. 54v, including Caxton's colophon.

locators.value: local repair on p. 1; pp. 72-75; pp. 99-101; pp. 167-205
notes 11-12; note 35 to note 36; note 52 to Harley milestone 33/1; note 97 through the colophon
local opening wording; e5v-e6v; g3v-g5r; l2r-n4v

notes: The long final supply includes Caxton's 1483 translation and 1484 printing colophon; it is not a recovered ending of the anonymous translation.
The exact physical copy is identified as British Library IB.55085 by the ownership inscriptions reproduced in Wright's edition and matched in de Ricci's census.

related_works.title: Livre pour l'enseignement de ses filles
notes: Geoffroy composed the French work in 1371-1372. Royal MS 19 C VII is a surviving French copy, but no reviewed evidence identifies it as the anonymous translator's physical exemplar.

related_works.title: The Knight of the Tower (William Caxton's translation)
notes: Caxton completed his distinct translation on 1 June 1483 and printed it at Westminster on 31 January 1484. Wright used selected Caxton passages as editorial substitutes for missing anonymous-translation text; the two English versions must not be merged as one intellectual work.

evidence.summary: Supplies the 1906 source edition statement, U-M encoding date and legacy terms, explicit Harley/Caxton dependency, editorial omissions and normalizations, preserved foreword and introduction, work text, Caxton passage markers, and chapter structure.

evidence.citation: General Corpus structural inspection of KntTour-L.xml.
evidence.locator: DIV1, DIV2, P, PB, MILESTONE, NOTE1, LG, and L counts
evidence.summary: Counts 135 DIV1, six DIV2, 186 P, 218 PB, 259 MILESTONE, 102 NOTE1, zero LG, and zero L elements; distinguishes two front divisions from 133 body divisions and 216 Harley folio-column from 43 Caxton-signature milestones.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, ‘Book of the Knight of La Tour-Landry,’ Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse.
evidence.summary: Confirms the current public item, Wright's 1906 edition metadata, encoded contents, and current public-domain / No Copyright-United States statement.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Harley MS 1764.
evidence.locator: title, scope, contents ff. 1r-54v, date, origin, physical description, access, and related-material note
evidence.summary: Catalogues the only known surviving copy of the anonymous earlier Middle English translation; dates the manuscript 1425-1474; locates production in England; records ff. 1r-54v, parchment, two columns, Gothic cursive, missing leaves after fols. 24 and 32, and an imperfect ending.

evidence.citation: Geoffroy de La Tour-Landry, The Book of the Knight of La Tour-Landry, ed. Thomas Wright, rev. ed., EETS Original Series 33 (London, 1906), complete scan.
evidence.locator: title page; Furnivall foreword pp. v-vi; Wright introduction pp. vii-xvi; text pp. 1-205; notes 11, 35, 52, 97; ownership inscriptions after colophon
evidence.summary: Establishes the revised edition, earlier anonymous translation, Harley base text, separate Caxton translation, passage-level supplies, revision history, and exact reproduced ownership inscriptions.

evidence.citation: Geoffroy de La Tour-Landry, The Book of the Knight of La Tour-Landry, ed. Thomas Wright, EETS Original Series 33 (London, 1868), complete first-edition scan.
evidence.summary: Confirms the 1868 first publication and its claim to edit the anonymous translation from the unique British Museum manuscript.

evidence.citation: Middle English Dictionary, quotations citing Knt.Tour-L.(Hrl 1764).
evidence.locator: quotation dates ?c1450 and short-title citation Knt.Tour-L.(Hrl 1764)
evidence.summary: Provides the established MED short title and conventional ?c1450 textual date used cautiously in the discovery metadata.

evidence.citation: CERL Incunabula Short Title Catalogue, il00072000, The Knight of the Tower [English], translated and printed by William Caxton.
evidence.locator: title, imprint, identifiers, related resources, and British Library holdings
evidence.summary: Records Westminster, William Caxton, 31 January 1484, STC 15296, and the two British Library copies C.11.c.6 = IB.55086 and IB.55085.

evidence.citation: Seymour de Ricci, A Census of Caxtons (Oxford, 1909), no. 63.
evidence.summary: Identifies British Museum IB.55085, formerly C.21.d.15, as the nearly perfect copy with early owner Thomas Lane; these marks match the ownership inscriptions printed at the end of Wright's edition.

open_questions.question: Can a modern linguistic or textual study narrow the anonymous translation's date, dialect, or place of composition beyond the conventional ?c1450 and broad English origin?
next_steps: Consult a modern critical edition or dedicated study of the earlier anonymous translation and distinguish linguistic localization from Harley MS 1764's production place.

open_questions.question: Which French manuscript or textual branch did the anonymous translator use?
next_steps: Consult source studies comparing the anonymous translation with the French manuscript tradition.
Do not identify Royal MS 19 C VII as the translator's exemplar without direct evidence.

open_questions.question: How closely did the lost ending of the anonymous translation correspond to Caxton's supplied ending?
next_steps: Treat the Caxton continuation as editorial substitution, not reconstruction of the anonymous wording.
Review any modern comparative edition for recoverable indirect evidence.

notes: The current British Library record supersedes Wright's hand-based claim that the manuscript itself proves a Henry VI-reign translation date; manuscript production and translation are kept distinct.

Wright's exact Caxton source copy is identified as British Library IB.55085 by matching the Thomas Lane inscriptions printed in the edition to de Ricci's copy census. This is strong copy-level evidence, not a claim that every surviving 1484 copy has identical annotations.
Edition, encoding, facsimile access, reproduction requests, and rights evidence are maintained in manifests/lineage/works/KntTour-L.json.
